text: The laparoscope reveals challenges in exposing the sigmoid mesentery due to a fatty and short small bowel mesentery. The patient is positioned in steep Trendelenburg with the left side elevated, but this provides minimal displacement of the small bowel. The tattoo marking the polypectomy site is visible on the colon. Attempts to reposition the omentum over the liver and flip small bowel loops kephalad offer little additional exposure. Large appendices epiploicae on the sigmoid colon further hinder exposure. The surgeon abandons medial to lateral dissection due to poor exposure and instead performs lateral mobilization of the sigmoid colon. The left ureter is identified and protected. The distal transection level is marked at the pelvic brim, and a window is created in the mesentery next to the bowel, which is then divided at this level.

The surgeon constructs a homemade hand port using a wound retractor and a glove to improve exposure of the sigmoid mesentery. A small incision is made, and the wound retractor is inserted and tightened. The surgeon's right hand is placed in the abdomen, allowing for hand assistance to expose, dissect, and divide the sigmoidal branches despite the presence of small bowel loops. The sigmoid colon is exteriorized and transected through the incision. The anvil of a 31mm circular stapler is sewn in with a purse-string suture, and a stapled end-to-end colorectal anastomosis is performed after re-insufflating pneumoperitoneum.
